meta.title: 2.4 Serverless Functions & Edge Runtimes
meta.description: Compare AWS Lambda's Firecracker isolation, Vercel Functions' Next.js-aware layer, and Cloudflare Workers' V8-isolate model — and understand why Make's choice of API route execution environment constrains which npm packages, runtime APIs, and cold-start budgets are available.

blocks.prompt:
Serverless means "no server to manage." But there's obviously a server somewhere. What's actually different about serverless, and why would Make care? Write your current understanding before reading.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module explains the three major serverless models — Lambda's Firecracker microVMs, Vercel Functions, and Cloudflare Workers' V8 isolates — and why the underlying isolation mechanism determines capability, not just performance. By the end, you'll understand why "use Cloudflare Workers for everything" is compelling but wrong, and why the framework a generated app uses implicitly chooses its deployment target.

Serverless functions and edge runtimes solve the cost and cold-start problems of containers for stateless workloads, but their runtime API restrictions and execution time limits mean the framework you choose to generate determines which serverless targets are actually viable — and that coupling is invisible until you try to move.

**The serverless model:**

In traditional server deployments, you provision a machine (or a container), your code runs continuously on it, and you're billed for uptime regardless of whether requests are coming in. Your Express app starts, listens on port 3000, and stays resident in memory for weeks.

In serverless, you deploy a function. The platform receives a request, provisions an execution environment, runs your function to completion, returns the response, and de-provisions (or freezes) the environment. You're billed for execution time measured in milliseconds, not for uptime. At zero traffic, cost is zero.

**The three major serverless models and their isolation mechanisms:**

**AWS Lambda** runs your code in a microVM (a lightweight virtual machine — a full VM with its own kernel but with minimal overhead, using Amazon's open-source Firecracker technology). Each Lambda execution gets a dedicated microVM with its own kernel, memory space, and network interface. This provides strong isolation — one tenant's code genuinely cannot interfere with another's. Cold start (the delay when provisioning a fresh environment from zero) is 100ms-1s for a warmed environment, up to 3s for a true cold start with a large deployment package. Warm Lambda instances are reused across invocations (within ~15 minutes of last use), which is why Lambda code can maintain module-level state between invocations — a common source of bugs for developers who don't understand this.

**Vercel Functions** are Lambda under the hood for Node.js, with a framework-aware layer on top. Vercel understands Next.js API routes, Remix route handlers, and SvelteKit endpoints — it handles the build and deployment plumbing so you don't write Lambdas directly. From a PM perspective, Vercel Functions are the deployment target when Make generates a Next.js app.

**Cloudflare Workers** use a fundamentally different isolation model: **V8 isolates** (isolated JavaScript execution contexts — the same mechanism browsers use to prevent one tab from reading another tab's memory). Rather than spinning up a VM or container, Cloudflare runs your JavaScript code directly in a V8 isolate. A V8 isolate is dramatically lighter than a VM: ~10KB of overhead versus tens of megabytes for a container. Cold start is essentially zero — sub-millisecond. The cost: V8 isolates enforce strict limits on what runtime APIs are available. No `fs`, limited `fetch` patterns, no long-running connections, execution time caps at 50ms (CPU) by default.

---

blocks.question: AWS Lambda uses Firecracker microVMs for isolation. Cloudflare Workers use V8 isolates. Both are called 'serverless.' What is the concrete product difference a PM must understand when choosing between them for Make's server-side functions?
blocks.answer: Firecracker microVMs support full Node.js — you can use `fs`, `child_process`, TCP connections, and any npm package. Cold start is 100ms–3s. V8 isolates have sub-millisecond cold start but enforce strict API restrictions: no `fs`, limited network APIs, 50ms CPU cap, no long-running connections. If Make generates Next.js Server Actions that use Node.js APIs, those functions can run on Lambda/Vercel but not on Cloudflare Workers. The isolation model isn't just a security property — it's a capability constraint that determines which generated code can run where.

blocks.content:
**Edge runtimes** are serverless functions deployed to a globally distributed network of Points of Presence (PoPs — geographically distributed data centers that serve users from a nearby location instead of routing all requests to a central server). Cloudflare has 300+ PoPs worldwide. Vercel has edge functions running in ~50 regions. The implication: a user in Tokyo gets a response from a node that might be 5-10ms away, not 150ms away from a US-based data center. For latency-sensitive operations — auth token validation, A/B test routing, personalization — this matters enormously.

**Why serverless matters for Make's generated apps:**

The apps Make generates are not static websites. They typically include API routes (for form submissions, data fetching, auth callbacks), server-side rendering (SSR — generating HTML on the server rather than in the browser, which is needed for SEO and fast initial page loads), and potentially server actions (for database mutations). These server-side behaviors need somewhere to run.

When Make deploys a user's app, the question "where do the server-side parts run" has architectural consequences:

- **Next.js App Router** is built assuming serverless deployment. Server Components, Server Actions, and API routes all assume they'll run in short-lived function invocations. State persists in a database, not in memory.
- **Remix** is more flexible — it has adapters for Node.js long-running servers, Cloudflare Workers, and Vercel. But the code you write differs by adapter.
- **SvelteKit** is adapter-based by design — you choose your deployment target and the adapter generates the appropriate output.

**The framework coupling problem:**

This is the part that's often invisible until you're locked in. When Make chooses a default framework, it implicitly chooses a serverless deployment target. Next.js → Vercel is the canonical pairing. Remix → Cloudflare Workers is increasingly common. These aren't just deployment preferences — the framework's server-side primitives are designed around the capabilities of the target runtime.

If Make defaults to Next.js and generates Server Actions, those Server Actions assume they can run for up to 60 seconds (Vercel's function timeout). Port that app to Cloudflare Workers and the 50ms CPU limit makes those Server Actions non-starters.

## How to Think About This

"Serverless" is a billing and operational model, not a capability model. Two products both labeled "serverless" — Cloudflare Workers and AWS Lambda — have completely different execution capabilities. Cloudflare Workers' V8 isolate cannot run Node.js APIs; Lambda's Firecracker microVM can run anything Linux runs. The practical rule: if the generated code uses Node.js-specific APIs (fs, child_process, native modules), only Lambda-class execution is viable, regardless of how appealing Workers' zero cold-start looks. The isolation mechanism is the product constraint, not just an implementation detail.

**The cold start problem in comparative perspective:**

| Platform | Isolation | Cold Start | Cost Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cloudflare Workers | V8 isolate | ~0ms | Per request |
| Vercel Edge Functions | V8 isolate | ~0ms | Per request |
| Vercel Node.js Functions | Lambda (Firecracker) | 100ms-1s | Per GB-second |
| AWS Lambda | Firecracker microVM | 100ms-3s | Per GB-second |
| Docker container | Linux namespaces | 2-10s | Per container-hour |

For Make's preview infrastructure, the cold start column is critical. For Make's production deployment infrastructure, the cost model column matters more (since production containers are long-lived).

---

blocks.question: A user generates a Next.js app with Server Actions that write to a SQLite file on disk. The app works perfectly in the Make preview. When they deploy, the Server Actions stop persisting data between requests. Why?
blocks.answer:
SQLite writing to disk assumes a persistent filesystem — the same process always has access to the same file. Serverless functions run in ephemeral execution environments; the "disk" between invocations is not the same disk. A Lambda or Vercel Function may run your code in any of dozens of containers, none of which share a filesystem. Data written to disk in one invocation is invisible to the next. The fix is to use a real database (PostgreSQL, PlanetScale, Turso) that persists externally. This is a case where the preview environment (which might use a persistent WebContainer filesystem) is misleading about production behavior.*

---

blocks.title:
Deploy the same "hello world" function to three platforms and measure cold start:

1. **Cloudflare Workers** (free tier) — note the sub-millisecond response even on first hit
2. **Vercel Functions** — create a Next.js API route, deploy, then wait 15 minutes of inactivity and hit it again
3. **AWS Lambda** — deploy via the console with a simple Node.js handler

Record the first-request latency after a cold period for each. These are real numbers you'll reference in engineering discussions.

---
blocks.solution: Cloudflare Workers should respond in under 5ms on first hit — the V8 isolate model eliminates meaningful cold start. You may struggle to observe any latency at all; that's the point. For Vercel Functions after 15+ minutes of inactivity, watch for a first request that takes 300ms–1.5 seconds, then subsequent requests dropping to 30–100ms — that gap is the cold start cost for spinning up a Lambda-backed environment. For AWS Lambda, deploy a simple Node.js handler and invoke it from the console after a cold period; 200ms–3 seconds is typical depending on deployment package size. If your function has large dependencies, the cold start will be longer. Record these numbers — they're real benchmarks for design discussions. Note that Cloudflare Workers' near-zero cold start comes with the V8 API restriction the module describes: if you try to add `require('fs')` to your Worker, it will fail, which is the capability tradeoff made visible.

blocks.prompt:
Your engineering lead says: "We should use Cloudflare Workers for all server-side execution in Make because cold starts are zero." In two paragraphs, explain why this is compelling but not straightforwardly correct.

---

questions:
As Platform PM, what is the business risk of coupling Make's deployment model tightly to either one of these platforms — and have you seen a similar vendor lock-in situation in a product you've worked on? What made the coupling visible, and what was the cost of unwinding it?

---

blocks.content:
Serverless functions and edge runtimes solve the cost and cold-start problems of containers for stateless workloads, but their runtime API restrictions and execution time limits mean the framework you choose to generate determines which serverless targets are actually viable — and that coupling is invisible until you try to move.

---

blocks.prompt:
The preview environment and the serverless deployment target both involve running untrusted or AI-generated code. But neither WebContainers nor serverless functions were designed primarily as security boundaries. What happens when the AI generates code that tries to do something harmful — and what platform-level mechanisms exist to stop it?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: What is the primary reason Cloudflare Workers achieve near-zero cold start while AWS Lambda has cold starts of 100ms–3 seconds?
options: Cloudflare Workers use V8 Isolates — lightweight JavaScript execution contexts around 10KB overhead — instead of microVMs that must boot a kernel and initialize a full process
Cloudflare has more data centers globally, so users are always served from a nearby node
AWS Lambda is older technology that hasn't been optimized; Cloudflare's architecture is newer and faster by design
reviewQuestions.explanation: The cold start difference comes from the isolation model. Cloudflare Workers use V8 Isolates — approximately 10KB overhead, no kernel boot required. AWS Lambda uses Firecracker microVMs — each invocation spins up a lightweight VM with its own kernel, which takes 100ms–3 seconds. Geographic distribution affects latency to the user but not cold start duration.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the isolation mechanism, not the number of data centers, is what determines cold start time.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The number of Cloudflare data centers affects how close users are to a node, but cold start time is about the isolation model. V8 Isolates (~10KB overhead) start in sub-milliseconds because there's no kernel or process to boot. Lambda's Firecracker microVMs must initialize a lightweight kernel and process, which takes 100ms–3 seconds.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following types of code CANNOT run on Cloudflare Workers, and why?
options: TypeScript compiled to JavaScript — Cloudflare Workers only support plain ES5
A Next.js Server Action that uses Node.js's fs module to read a file
A fetch-based API call to an external REST endpoint
reviewQuestions.explanation: Cloudflare Workers use V8 Isolates with a deliberately restricted API surface: no fs, no child_process, no arbitrary TCP connections. A Server Action using fs is a Node.js API call — it will fail in the V8 Isolate model regardless of cold-start advantages. fetch is supported. TypeScript compiled to JavaScript is fine.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good — this is the capability tradeoff at the heart of the Workers vs. Lambda decision. Sub-millisecond cold start comes at the cost of Node.js API access.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Cloudflare Workers support modern JavaScript and TypeScript (compiled), and fetch calls work fine. What they cannot run is code using Node.js-specific APIs like fs, child_process, or native npm modules — because those require OS-level access that V8 Isolates deliberately exclude.

reviewQuestions.question: A user generates a Next.js app with a Server Action that writes data to a local SQLite file. It works in the Make preview but data disappears between requests in production. What causes this?
options: SQLite is not supported as a Vercel dependency — users must use PostgreSQL or MySQL
The Make preview uses a different version of Node.js than Vercel Functions, causing SQLite file format incompatibility
Serverless function instances are ephemeral — each invocation may run on a different container with its own temporary filesystem, so data written to disk in one invocation is not visible to the next
reviewQuestions.explanation: Serverless function instances are ephemeral. Each invocation may be served by a different container, each with a fresh filesystem that is discarded when the instance terminates. The preview environment (a persistent session) creates the illusion of a durable filesystem. In serverless production, local disk writes don't persist between requests. The fix is an external persistent database.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it — the ephemeral filesystem is the classic preview-to-production parity failure for serverless, and it's important for Make's Platform PM to understand why it happens.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Vercel supports SQLite and Node.js version differences aren't the issue. The problem is ephemeral execution instances: each serverless invocation may run on a different container with a fresh filesystem. Data written to local disk in one request simply isn't there for the next. An external database is required for persistence in serverless.
